input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

input.initialPrompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 9+, `System.Text.Json` added `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` as a built-in naming policy. Since the record has lowercase parameter names (`name`, `age`), the C# compiler generates properties with those same lowercase names. To get PascalCase output (`Name`, `Age`), we can use `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase`.

Wait, let me think about this more carefully. In C#, when you declare `public record Person(string name, int age)`, the compiler generates properties `name` and `age` (lowercase, matching the parameter names). 

I need to verify when `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` was actually introduced. The built-in policies I'm aware of are `CamelCase` from .NET Core 3.0 and `SnakeCaseLower` from .NET 8, but I'm uncertain about PascalCase's availability.

Let me think through this differently—since record parameters are lowercase, the generated properties will also be lowercase by default, matching the parameter names exactly.

The user could simply capitalize the property names in the record definition itself, but they've specifically defined it with lowercase and want PascalCase JSON output. Let me just try creating a project with .NET 11 and attempt to use `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` to see if it's available. I should first check what SDKs are actually installed on the system.

data.content: Good — .NET 11 Preview 3 SDK is available. Let me create the project and test it.
